section.title: The Lord's Anger against Zion
section.sectionRationale: The second poem lifts its eyes from the ruined city to the hand behind the ruin. Where chapter one wept over what happened, chapter two dares to say who did it: the Lord Himself, in the day of His anger. It is the most unflinching statement in the book that Jerusalem's fall was the deliberate act of God against His own people for their sin, and yet it ends by teaching them to pour out their hearts to that same Lord.
section.thesis: Jerusalem's destruction was no accident of history but the deliberate work of the Lord in righteous anger against His people's sin; yet even the God who wielded the sword is the God to whom the poet directs His people to lift up their hands and pour out their hearts.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the Lord as warrior against Zion, tearing down temple, wall, and king, 2:1 to 10; the poet's overwhelming grief at the suffering of the people, especially the starving children, 2:11 to 19; and the closing cry flung back to the Lord who caused it, 2:20 to 22.

units.label: The Lord has become like an enemy

verses.text: How the Lord has covered the Daughter of Zion with the cloud of His {{aph|anger}}! He has cast the glory of Israel from heaven to earth. He has abandoned His footstool in the day of His anger.

Without {{chamal|pity}} the Lord has {{bala|swallowed up}} all the dwellings of Jacob. In His wrath He has demolished the fortified cities of the Daughter of Judah. He brought to the ground and defiled her kingdom and its princes.
In fierce anger He has cut off every horn of Israel and withdrawn His right hand at the approach of the enemy. He has burned in Jacob like a flaming fire that consumes everything around it.
He has bent His bow like an enemy; His right hand is positioned. Like a foe He has killed all who were pleasing to the eye; He has poured out His wrath like fire on the tent of the Daughter of Zion.

The Lord is like an enemy; He has {{bala|swallowed up}} Israel. He has swallowed up all her palaces and destroyed her strongholds. He has multiplied mourning and lamentation for the Daughter of Judah.

He has laid waste His tabernacle like a garden booth; He has destroyed His place of meeting. The LORD has made Zion forget her appointed feasts and Sabbaths. In His fierce anger He has despised both king and priest.

The Lord has rejected His altar; He has abandoned His sanctuary; He has delivered the walls of her palaces into the hand of the enemy. They have raised a shout in the house of the LORD as on the day of an appointed feast.

The LORD determined to destroy the wall of the Daughter of Zion. He stretched out a measuring line and did not withdraw His hand from destroying. He made the ramparts and walls lament; together they waste away.

Her gates have sunk into the ground; He has destroyed and shattered their bars. Her king and her princes are exiled among the nations, the law is no more, and even her prophets find no vision from the LORD.
The elders of the Daughter of Zion sit on the ground in silence. They have thrown dust on their heads and put on sackcloth. The young women of Jerusalem have bowed their heads to the ground.
My eyes fail from weeping; I am churning within. My heart is poured out in grief over the destruction of the daughter of my people, because children and infants faint in the streets of the city.
They cry out to their mothers: “Where is the grain and wine?” as they faint like the wounded in the streets of the city, as their lives fade away in the arms of their mothers.

What can I say for you? To what can I compare you, O Daughter of Jerusalem? To what can I liken you, that I may console you, O Virgin Daughter of Zion? For your wound is as deep as the sea. Who can ever heal you?
The visions of your prophets were empty and deceptive; they did not expose your guilt to ward off your captivity. The burdens they envisioned for you were empty and misleading.
All who pass by clap their hands at you in scorn. They hiss and shake their heads at the Daughter of Jerusalem: “Is this the city that was called the perfection of beauty, the joy of all the earth?”
All your enemies open their mouths against you. They hiss and gnash their teeth, saying, “We have swallowed her up. This is the day for which we have waited. We have lived to see it!”

The LORD has done what He planned; He has accomplished His decree, which He ordained in days of old; He has overthrown you without {{chamal|pity}}. He has let the enemy gloat over you and exalted the horn of your foes.
The hearts of the people cry out to the Lord. O wall of the Daughter of Zion, let your tears run down like a river day and night. Give yourself no relief, and your eyes no rest.

Arise, cry out in the night from the first watch of the night. Pour out your heart like water in the presence of the Lord. Lift up your hands to Him for the lives of your children who are fainting from hunger on the corner of every street.

units.label: Look, O LORD, and consider
units.note: the cry flung back to God

verses.text: Look, O LORD, and consider: Whom have You ever treated like this? Should women eat their offspring, the infants they have nurtured? Should priests and prophets be killed in the sanctuary of the Lord?

Both young and old lie together in the dust of the streets. My young men and maidens have fallen by the sword. You have slain them in the day of Your {{aph|anger}}; You have slaughtered them without compassion.
You summoned my terrors on every side, as for the day of an appointed feast. In the day of the LORD's anger no one escaped or survived; my enemy has destroyed those I nurtured and reared.

aph.gloss: Anger, wrath; literally the nose or nostril, from the flaring or hot breathing of anger. The chapter is saturated with it: the cloud of His anger, the day of His anger, His fierce anger. It names God's settled, righteous opposition to sin, not a loss of temper.

chamal.gloss: To spare, to show pity, to hold back. The refrain without pity, in 2:2, 2:17, and 2:21, is chilling precisely because sparing mercy is God's normal way. That He did not spare shows how serious persistent, unrepented sin had become.

bala.gloss: To swallow up, to engulf and destroy without trace. Used repeatedly of the Lord swallowing up Israel's dwellings, palaces, and strongholds, it pictures total, irreversible ruin, the same word the gloating enemies then throw back, we have swallowed her up.

basic.context: If chapter one wept over the ruins, chapter two names who brought them down: the Lord. This is the hardest chapter to read, because verse after verse says that God Himself, in righteous anger, tore down His own city, His temple, His altar, His king. He became, the poet says, like an enemy. The poem does not soften this. Then it turns to the agony on the ground: starving children fainting in the streets, crying to their mothers for bread that is not there. And it ends by flinging the whole horror back to the God who caused it, Look, O LORD, and consider.
basic.hermeneutics: We have to let this chapter be as severe as it is. It refuses the comfort of saying the disaster just happened or that Babylon simply won. It insists God did this, and did it justly, for real sin, including the false prophets who never called the people to repent. But notice where the poem sends its grief: not away from God in bitterness, but straight to God in prayer. The startling lesson is that the same Lord who wounded is the only Lord who can heal, so pour out your heart to Him.
basic.originalLanguages: The word for anger, aph, drenches the chapter: the cloud of His anger, the day of His anger, His fierce anger. It pictures God's hot, settled opposition to sin, not a tantrum. The chilling refrain without pity translates chamal, to spare, the very thing God normally does, now withheld. And again and again the Lord has swallowed up, bala, His people, a word for total ruin that the enemies then gloat back: we have swallowed her up.
basic.typology: The Lord not sparing His own city and pouring out His wrath foreshadows the deeper mystery of the cross, where God did not spare His own Son but delivered Him up for us all. The wound as deep as the sea, with the cry Who can ever heal you, waits for the one by whose wounds we are healed. And the ruined temple points to Christ, the true temple, torn down and raised again.
basic.christ: The unbearable question of this chapter, Who can ever heal you, is answered only in Christ. On the cross God did not spare His own Son, pouring out on Him the anger our sin deserved, so that the wound as deep as the sea could finally be healed. Jesus is the true temple that was destroyed and rebuilt in three days, and the true King who goes into exile, into death, that His people might come home.
basic.god: God is shown as terrifyingly holy and utterly sovereign: nothing here is outside His hand, and His anger against sin is real and just. Yet even this fierce chapter assumes He is approachable, ending in direct appeal to Him. He is not capricious; He does what He planned and said He would do. And the fact that the poem prays to Him at all confesses that mercy is still possible with the God who judged.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 28 warned of exactly this judgment for covenant breaking. Jeremiah 23 exposes the false prophets who said peace when there was no peace. Romans 8 says God did not spare His own Son. John 2 shows Jesus as the temple destroyed and raised. 1 Peter 2 says by His wounds we are healed.
basic.oneStory: This is the sharpest edge of the exile: God Himself judging His people's sin. But even here the door is not shut. The poem drives the grief back to God in prayer, and the whole story runs on to the cross, where God does not spare His own Son, absorbing the anger of this chapter so that the incurable wound can be healed.

context.literary: The second acrostic poem, again twenty two verses following the Hebrew alphabet. It intensifies chapter one by making God the explicit subject of relentless verbs of destruction, He has swallowed up, demolished, cut off, burned, rejected, abandoned. It moves from the Lord as warrior, 2:1 to 10, to the poet's grief, 2:11 to 19, to a final direct address to God, 2:20 to 22.
context.historical: It reflects the Babylonian siege and sack of Jerusalem in 586 BC: the breaching of the wall, the burning of the temple, the exile of King Zedekiah and the princes, and the famine so severe that mothers are described eating their own children, the horror foretold in the covenant curses. The false prophets named in 2:14 had assured the city of peace and safety.
context.canonical: Chapter two is Scripture's frank testimony that God judges sin even among His own covenant people, fulfilling Deuteronomy 28. Its imagery of a God who did not spare and of a wound none can heal reaches forward to the New Testament, where God does not spare His Son and provides the healing no human comforter could.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To confess without evasion that the Lord Himself, justly and deliberately, brought the catastrophe on Zion for her sin and her prophets' lies, and yet to direct the grieving people to pour out that very grief in prayer to the God who wounded them, as their only hope of healing.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The poem describes God's judgment and the people's suffering; it does not prescribe the atrocities of the siege, which it laments with horror. What it prescribes, in 2:18 to 19, is the response: to cry out day and night, to pour out the heart like water before the Lord, and to lift up hands to Him for the vulnerable.

debates.question: Should we say plainly that God destroyed Jerusalem, or was it merely the Babylonians?

views.label: God is the true agent of the judgment
views.case: The poem is emphatic and unembarrassed: the Lord has swallowed up, demolished, cut off, and become like an enemy, 2:1 to 8; He did what He planned and ordained in days of old, 2:17. The disaster is His righteous act, not a mere geopolitical event.

views.label: Babylon is the instrument, God the governor
views.case: The Babylonians really besieged and burned the city; Scripture elsewhere calls them God's instrument, so God ordains and governs the judgment while using human agents who remain accountable for their cruelty, which the poem also condemns.

debates.bearing: Both are held together: God is genuinely sovereign over the judgment and just in it, while the human enemies who gloat, 2:16, are still guilty. Confessing God's hand keeps the sufferer praying to Him rather than despairing.

debates.question: How does the poem view Zion's suffering, as fully deserved or as also protest?

views.label: Deserved judgment for sin and false prophecy
views.case: The prophets' empty visions failed to expose guilt or ward off captivity, 2:14, and the Lord accomplished the decree He had long ordained, 2:17; the ruin is the just wage of persistent sin.

views.label: Anguished protest that still questions the severity
views.case: The raw questions of 2:20, Whom have You ever treated like this, should women eat their offspring, are not cool acceptance but a wounded plea that lays the horror before God and asks Him to see it.

debates.bearing: Faithful lament holds both: the judgment is just, and the pain is real enough to bring honest, even shocking, questions straight to God, which is itself an act of faith that He is listening.

study.originalLanguages: The pulse of the chapter is aph, anger, literally the flaring nostril of wrath: the cloud of His anger, the day of His anger, His fierce anger. This is not divine bad temper but settled, righteous opposition to sin. The most haunting word is chamal, to spare or pity, in the refrain without pity, for sparing is exactly what God usually does; its absence measures how grave unrepented sin had become. And over and over the Lord has swallowed up, bala, His people and their strongholds, a word for engulfing, traceless ruin that the enemies then hurl back in triumph, we have swallowed her up.

typology.type: The Lord who did not spare His own city, 2:2, 2:17
typology.fulfillment: The Father who did not spare His own Son but gave Him up for us all, Romans 8:32, bearing the anger this chapter describes.

typology.type: The wound as deep as the sea, Who can ever heal you, 2:13
typology.fulfillment: Christ, by whose wounds we are healed, 1 Peter 2:24, the only cure for a wound no human comforter could touch.

typology.type: The destroyed temple and rejected altar, 2:6 to 7
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, the true temple destroyed and raised in three days, John 2:19 to 21, and the final sacrifice that reconciles.

study.christ: This chapter presses a question no one in Jerusalem could answer: your wound is as deep as the sea, who can ever heal you? The gospel answers it. On the cross God did not spare His own Son but poured out on Him the fierce anger, the aph, that our sin deserved, so that the incurable wound might be healed by His wounds. Jesus is the true temple that was torn down and raised, and the true anointed King who went into the exile of death to bring His people home. The Lord who became like an enemy to Zion made Himself an enemy of our sin at the cross, and there, and only there, becomes our peace.
study.god: God is revealed as holy, sovereign, and just: the destruction is His deliberate act, accomplished exactly as He had long decreed, and His anger against persistent sin is neither arbitrary nor excessive but righteous. Yet the same chapter that calls Him an enemy also prays to Him, which confesses that He is still Israel's God and still able to hear. His refusal to spare is terrible, but it is the seriousness of a holy God about sin, the very seriousness that, at the cross, becomes the measure of His mercy.

crossReferences.note: The covenant curse of siege and famine, even to eating one's own children, now horrifyingly fulfilled.
The false prophets who spoke visions of their own minds and said peace, condemned in Lamentations 2:14.
God did not spare His own Son but gave Him up for us all, the gospel answer to a God who did not spare.
Jesus is the temple that would be destroyed and raised, fulfilling the ruined temple of Zion.
By His wounds you have been healed, the cure for the wound as deep as the sea.

study.oneStory: Chapter two is the darkest confession in the book: the Lord Himself, in righteous anger, tore down His own city for its sin. Yet even here the poem prays, refusing to let go of God. The whole Bible answers its unbearable question, Who can ever heal you, at the cross, where the Father does not spare His own Son but lets the anger fall on Him, so that the incurable wound of His people can be healed and the true temple raised.

sermon.title: Suffering in the Believer's Life
sermon.why: Listed on Truth For Life's own collection of Alistair Begg's Lamentations sermons as a message on Lamentations 2:20, this exposition handles the suffering and the cry to God at the heart of this chapter in a faithful, Christ centered way.

quiz.q: What is the most striking claim of Lamentations 2?

options.text: That the Lord Himself, in righteous anger against sin, brought the destruction on Zion and became like an enemy to her.
options.note: The poem makes God the explicit subject of the destruction, 2:1 to 8 and 2:17.

options.text: That God was angry with Jerusalem and judged her.
options.note: True, though it understates how directly and relentlessly the poem names God as the agent.

options.text: That the Babylonians acted entirely on their own against God's will.
options.note: Babylon was the instrument, but the poem insists the Lord ordained and accomplished the judgment.

options.text: That the disaster was a random misfortune with no cause.
options.note: The poem gives it a clear cause: God's just judgment on sin.

quiz.q: What does the chilling refrain without pity, chamal, tell us?

options.text: That God withheld the sparing mercy He normally shows, because persistent, unrepented sin had become so grave.
options.note: Chamal means to spare; its absence measures the seriousness of the sin.

options.text: That God acted without holding back His judgment.
options.note: Correct sense, though it misses that sparing is precisely God's usual way, now withheld.

options.text: That God felt nothing at all about His people.
options.note: The chapter is full of God's engagement; the point is withheld sparing, not indifference.

options.text: That God was cruel and unjust.
options.note: The poem presents the judgment as just, the accomplishing of what He had long decreed.

quiz.q: What does the poet urge the people to do with their grief, in 2:18 to 19?

options.text: To cry out day and night and pour out their hearts like water in the presence of the Lord, lifting up their hands to Him.
options.note: The grief is directed straight back to God in prayer, not away from Him.

options.text: To keep praying and weeping before God without rest.
options.note: True, and specifically to pour out the heart like water and lift hands to Him for the vulnerable.

options.text: To give up on God and accept that all is lost.
options.note: The poem does the opposite: it turns the grief into persistent prayer.

options.text: To take revenge on the Babylonians themselves.
options.note: It calls for crying out to God, leaving judgment to Him.

quiz.q: How does the wound as deep as the sea, Who can ever heal you, point to Christ?

options.text: The incurable wound is healed only in Christ, by whose wounds His people are healed, since God did not spare His own Son.
options.note: The unanswerable question of 2:13 finds its answer at the cross.

options.text: It shows a suffering so deep that only God could heal it.
options.note: True, and Scripture names that healing specifically in Christ's wounds, 1 Peter 2:24.

options.text: It means Jerusalem's wound would never be healed at all.
options.note: The gospel provides the healing the chapter longs for; it is not left hopeless forever.

options.text: It has nothing to do with Christ.
options.note: The New Testament reads the healing of an incurable wound as fulfilled in Christ.

quiz.q: How can the same God who wounded Zion still be prayed to in this chapter?

options.text: Because the God who justly judged is the only God who can heal, so the poem drives its grief straight to Him.
options.note: Confessing God's hand keeps the sufferer praying rather than despairing.

options.text: Because He is still their God and able to hear their cry.
options.note: True, and more fully because only the God who wounded can also heal.

options.text: Because they hoped God had not really been involved.
options.note: The poem is clear that God was involved; it prays to Him anyway.

options.text: Because prayer here is just empty habit.
options.note: The prayers are urgent and heartfelt, an act of faith that God listens.

questions: This chapter says plainly that God was behind Jerusalem's fall. Is it harder or more comforting for you to believe that nothing, even disaster, falls outside God's hand? Why?
The false prophets comforted the people with visions of peace instead of exposing their guilt. Where are you tempted to prefer reassuring lies over hard truth about your own life?
The poet does not bury his grief or aim it at the enemy; he pours it out like water before the Lord. What would it look like to bring your rawest sorrow directly to God in prayer this week?
Your wound is as deep as the sea, who can ever heal you? Where in your life does the wound feel beyond healing, and how does the cross speak to it?
God did not spare His own Son so that His people would be spared. How does knowing the anger of this chapter fell on Jesus change the way you rest in God's love?

translationNotes.note: His footstool refers to the temple or the ark, the earthly place of God's presence; to abandon His footstool is to give up His own dwelling to destruction.
Every horn can also be rendered all the strength; the horn is a common biblical image of power, so cutting off the horn means breaking Israel's strength.
The opening line is literally Their heart cries out to the Lord; the sense is that the people's hearts cry out, which the translation makes explicit.
The first watch of the night refers to the earliest of the night watches, roughly the hours between six and nine in the evening, the beginning of a whole night of crying out.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Lamentations 2:1-22. Verse segments concatenated in order and separated by single spaces; poetic lines rendered as flowing prose within each verse. No em or en dashes present in this chapter's source; none used anywhere in the file.
